questions.question: The first moon landing was achieved by which country in 1969?
questions.explanation: NASA's Apollo 11 mission landed astronauts Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin on the Moon on July 20, 1969. Armstrong was the first to step on the surface, saying 'That's one small step for man, one giant leap for mankind.' Michael Collins orbited the Moon in the command module. The mission fulfilled President Kennedy's 1961 pledge to land a man on the Moon before the end of the decade.
questions.memory_hook: Moon landing = USA, July 20, 1969. Armstrong first, Aldrin second, Collins in orbit. 'One small step for man.' Kennedy set the goal in 1961 — they made it with 5 months to spare.

questions.question: The Great Depression, one of the worst economic crises in history, began with a stock market crash in which year?
questions.explanation: The Great Depression began with the stock market crash of October 1929 (Black Tuesday), when stock prices collapsed catastrophically. By 1932, US unemployment reached 25%. The Depression spread globally, lasted through most of the 1930s, and helped fuel the political instability that led to World War II. It ended largely due to the massive government spending associated with WWII.
questions.memory_hook: Great Depression = 1929 crash, Black Tuesday. 25% unemployment. The prosperity of the 1920s collapsed almost overnight. The Depression changed how governments think about their role in managing economies.

questions.question: Which U.S. president authorized the dropping of atomic bombs on Hiroshima and Nagasaki in August 1945, ending World War II in the Pacific?
questions.explanation: Harry Truman became president on April 12, 1945, following FDR's death — just weeks before Germany surrendered. He authorized the use of atomic bombs on Hiroshima (August 6) and Nagasaki (August 9), 1945. Japan surrendered on August 15, ending World War II. Truman also launched the Marshall Plan to rebuild postwar Europe.
questions.memory_hook: Truman = atomic bombs, 1945. Became president when FDR died.

questions.question: Dr. James Naismith invented basketball in 1891 at a YMCA in Springfield, Massachusetts. What was his nationality?
questions.explanation: James Naismith was born in Almonte, Ontario, Canada in 1861. He created basketball as an indoor winter activity while working as a physical education instructor in Springfield, Massachusetts, using peach baskets as goals. He became a US citizen only in 1925 — more than 30 years after inventing the sport. The NBA was founded in 1946, and basketball is now played by an estimated 450 million people worldwide.
questions.memory_hook: Naismith was Canadian, not American. He invented basketball in the US but was born in Ontario. Canada quietly gave the world one of its biggest sports — people are always surprised.

questions.question: In Formula 1 racing, how many points does the winner of a race receive?
questions.explanation: The winner of a Formula 1 Grand Prix receives 25 points in the current scoring system (introduced in 2010). Second place gets 18, third gets 15, decreasing to 1 point for 10th place. An additional point is awarded for the fastest lap (if the driver finishes in the top 10). The driver with the most points at season's end wins the World Drivers' Championship.
questions.memory_hook: F1 win = 25 points. Introduced in 2010. Before that, wins were worth 10. The current system rewards consistency — 25 for a win, 18 for second, down to 1 for 10th. Second place pays well in Formula 1.

questions.question: Which Seattle grunge band, fronted by Kurt Cobain, released 'Nevermind' in 1991 and brought alternative rock to mainstream audiences?
questions.explanation: Nirvana's 'Nevermind,' released September 13, 1991, knocked Michael Jackson's 'Dangerous' off the top of the Billboard 200. The album's lead single 'Smells Like Teen Spirit' became an anthem of Generation X. Cobain's suicide in 1994 at age 27 made him one of rock's most iconic tragic figures.
questions.memory_hook: Nirvana, Nevermind, 1991. Cobain knocked Michael Jackson off #1.

questions.question: Jean-Michel Basquiat rose from painting graffiti on Manhattan streets as 'SAMO' to selling work for millions — all before his death at 27. What makes his art historically significant?
options: He was the first Black artist to have solo exhibitions at both MoMA and the Guggenheim simultaneously, breaking institutional barriers that had excluded Black artists for decades

He merged graffiti and street art with Neo-Expressionist painting, incorporating text, symbols, and Black history into raw canvases that confronted racism and power — while achieving commercial art-world success as a young Black man in the 1980s
He invented a painting technique using actual street materials — pavement, tar, newspaper — that transformed found-object art and inspired the Young British Artists movement of the 1990s
He was discovered by Andy Warhol, who trained him for three years before Basquiat exceeded his mentor's commercial success and became the first artist to outsell Warhol at auction
questions.answer: He merged graffiti and street art with Neo-Expressionist painting, incorporating text, symbols, and Black history into raw canvases that confronted racism and power — while achieving commercial art-world success as a young Black man in the 1980s
questions.explanation: Basquiat grew up in Brooklyn, taught himself art history from a library copy of Gray's Anatomy given to him by his mother, and began writing cryptic SAMO ('same old shit') graffiti in SoHo at 17. By 21 he was shown in the Venice Biennale. His paintings fuse crowns, anatomy, copyright symbols, historical Black figures, and fragmented text into an intensely personal visual language about race, identity, and genius. He and Andy Warhol collaborated and became close friends. He died of a heroin overdose in 1988. A 1982 painting sold for $110.5 million in 2017.
questions.memory_hook: Basquiat went from spray-painting SAMO on SoHo walls at 17 to the Venice Biennale at 21 — his paintings fused street art, Black history, anatomy diagrams, and text into raw confrontations with racism. He died at 27; a 1982 canvas sold for $110 million.

questions.question: What Pixar film features a rat who dreams of becoming a gourmet chef in Paris?
questions.explanation: Ratatouille (2007) was directed by Brad Bird and tells the story of Remy, a rat with an extraordinary sense of taste and smell who aspires to become a chef in a Parisian restaurant. The film won the Academy Award for Best Animated Feature and is frequently praised for its sophisticated themes about creativity, class, and the nature of criticism.
questions.memory_hook: Ratatouille = rat chef in Paris. Won the Oscar for Best Animated Feature. The villain food critic writes a final review that begins: 'Not everyone can become a great artist, but a great artist can come from anywhere.' Even Pixar films have philosophy.

questions.question: Which two continents are connected by the Isthmus of Panama?
questions.explanation: The Isthmus of Panama is a narrow strip of land connecting North and South America, separating the Pacific and Atlantic oceans. Before the Panama Canal (opened 1914), ships had to sail around Cape Horn. The isthmus is also the reason no land animals could travel between the continents before it formed about 3 million years ago — which is why South American and North American wildlife evolved so differently.
questions.memory_hook: Panama = the bridge between North and South America. Before it formed 3 million years ago, the Americas were separate. The Great American Interchange happened when animals crossed — North American predators wiped out many South American species.

questions.question: Most stars in the Milky Way are not solitary like our Sun — they exist in binary or multiple star systems. What have binary stars revealed about stellar physics?
options: Binary stars proved that stellar fusion is not the primary energy source for stars — instead, gravitational interaction between binary partners provides 60% of a typical star's observed luminosity

Binary stars allow precise measurement of stellar masses (impossible for solitary stars) because orbital mechanics let astronomers directly calculate each star's mass from orbital period and separation
Binary stars proved that solar systems around other stars are impossible — any planets would be gravitationally ejected by the complex gravitational field of two orbiting stars
Binary stars are the source of all heavy elements in the universe — only stars with a gravitational partner can generate the internal pressure needed to fuse elements heavier than iron
questions.answer: Binary stars allow precise measurement of stellar masses (impossible for solitary stars) because orbital mechanics let astronomers directly calculate each star's mass from orbital period and separation
questions.explanation: For solitary stars, mass can only be estimated from luminosity via theoretical models. But for binary systems, Newton's form of Kepler's Third Law lets astronomers calculate masses directly from observational data — orbital period and separation distance. Binary stars also produce phenomena impossible in single stars: Type Ia supernovae (used to measure dark energy) occur when a white dwarf in a binary system accretes enough mass from its companion to exceed the Chandrasekhar limit; neutron star mergers (gravitational wave sources) happen in binary systems. About 50% of Sun-like stars are in binary systems.
questions.memory_hook: Binary stars are nature's scales — orbital mechanics let astronomers calculate precise stellar masses that would be impossible to measure for solitary stars. Type Ia supernovae (the key to discovering dark energy) require binary stars: a white dwarf stealing mass from a companion until it explodes.

questions.question: The Yellow Sea, a semi-enclosed sea with unusually shallow, nutrient-rich water, lies between China and which peninsula?
questions.explanation: The Yellow Sea lies between China and the Korean Peninsula, connecting to the East China Sea to the south. It gets its name from the yellow-brown silt carried into it by the Yellow River (Huang He). Averaging only about 44 meters deep, it is one of the world's shallowest seas and has enormous tidal ranges in some areas (over 9 meters). It is one of the world's most productive fishing areas and has been an important trade route between China and Korea for thousands of years.
questions.memory_hook: Yellow Sea: between China and the Korean Peninsula. Named for yellow silt from the Yellow River. Extremely shallow (avg 44m) — huge tidal ranges. One of the world's most productive fisheries. China-Korea trade route for thousands of years.

questions.question: Underground reservoirs of fresh water stored in porous rock formations, from which wells and springs draw water, are called what?
questions.explanation: Aquifers are underground layers of porous rock, gravel, sand, or silt that hold groundwater. Confined aquifers are trapped between layers of impermeable rock; unconfined aquifers are recharged directly from surface water above. The Ogallala Aquifer under the American Great Plains is one of the world's largest, supplying 30% of US groundwater used for irrigation. Many aquifers worldwide are being depleted faster than they recharge due to over-pumping for agriculture and cities.
questions.memory_hook: Aquifers = underground freshwater reservoirs in porous rock. Wells drill down to them. The Ogallala Aquifer supplies 30% of US groundwater — pumped out over thousands of years, being drained in decades. Once gone, it takes centuries to refill. Many cities and farms depend on water that won't come back in our lifetimes.
